coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots modified MoS2 for high-efficiency and long-endurance persulfate activation: Enhanced electron transfer and piezoelectricity
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shule
Xu, Zhemi
Ji, Tianhao
Guan, Peiyuan
Weng, Yunxuan
coredata.dc:description: Persulfate (PS) or peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activated sulfate radicals can significantly enhance advanced oxidation processes owing to their high oxidation potentials. However, highly efficient and continuous activation of PS or PMS has been limited owing to the sluggish redox cycle. Herein, a photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalyst, Carbon quantum dots modified MoS2 nanosheets (MoS2/C-dots), has been developed to boost the electron transfer for PMS activation, and achieved significant antibiotics degradation (85.06 % degradation under weak visible-light and simulated water flow within 10 min, which is 3.83 times higher than that with PMS). The advance of MoS2/C-dots fabrication is four-fold: a. the attached C-dots on MoS2 improved the asymmetry of MoS2 and enhanced its piezoelectricity, which accelerated the photogenerated e− and hole separation, and hence, improved its piezo-photocatalytic activity; b. C-dots surface charge promotes the electron transfer to MoS2, and provides e− for PMS activation; c. C-dots may act as e− banks for continuous PMS activation, hence, improving the PMS activation endurance; d. the photo-piezocatalysts can synergistically utilize the abundant weak visible light and the natural mechanical energy such as tide and water flow for highly efficient pollution degradation in wastewater and also exhibit good cycling stability. In addition, free radical trapping experiments were employed to reveal the PMS activation mechanism, and the results showed that h+, 1O2, SO4 ·−, and ·OH were the main active species and a possible reaction pathway has been proposed. This work provides a rational piezo-photocatalyst design strategy for high-efficiency PS or PMS activation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MoS2/C-dots
Persulfate activation
Photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalysis
Antibiotic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism analysis of reactive species based on free radicals

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Research on Plastic Biodegradation Mechanism and Establishment of Consistency Evaluation System in Different Water Environments
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: S. Zhang and Z. Xu acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52302160) and the Beijing Municipal Education Commission (Grant No. KM202310011007). Y. Weng acknowledge financial support from Research on Plastic Biodegradation Mechanism and Establishment of Consistency Evaluation System in Different Water Environments (Grant No. 52073004).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of C-dots, MoS2 and MoS2/C-dots.
(a, b) SEM images and (c–h) EDS mapping of MoS2/C-dots.
TEM images of (a, b) MoS2 and (c, d) MoS2/C-dots.
(a) I-t curves, (b) EIS and (c) PL spectra of MoS2 and MoS2/C-dots.

(a) TC (12 mg/L) degradation with MoS2 (45 mg) and MoS2/C-dots (45 mg); (b) the k with time under different conditions (adsorption, weak visible-light irradiation, piezocatalysis, and weak visible-light irradiation + piezocatalysis).
(a–c) TC degradations and (a*–c*) the k with time by different systems: (a–a*) weak visible-light irradiation, (b–b*) piezocatalysis, (c–c*) weak visible-light irradiation + piezocatalysis.

Cycling tests of the TC degradation with (a) MoS2 + PMS and (b) MoS2/C-dots + PMS under piezoelectric condition (stirring: 700 rpm); the anti-interference ability evaluation of (c) MoS2 and (d) MoS2/C-dots with organic matter and protein.
The anti-interference ability evaluation of the catalysts with (a–b) inorganic anions and (c–d) different pH values.

Capture experiments of active species (TBA-2 mM, EDTA-2Na-2 mM, L-AA-2 mM, FFA-2 mM, Ethanol-2 mM) under different catalytic conditions: (a, d) Weak visible-light irradiation, (b, e) piezocatalysis, (c, f) weak visible-light irradiation + piezocatalysis with different catalysts: (a–c) MoS2 + PMS, (d–f) MoS2/C-dots + PMS.
(a) TC degradation performance with different scavengers; EPR spectra of (b) h+, (c) SO4 2− (d) O2 ·− and (e) 1O2 in piezocatalytic process. Conditions: PMS = 0.5 g/L, MoS2 or MoS2/C-dots = 0.45 g/L.
The schematic of PMS activation and TC degradation with MoS2/C-dots under photo-piezoelectric synergistic condition.
(a) MDZ and (b) CIP degradations by different catalysts.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots modified MoS2 for high-efficiency and long-endurance persulfate activation: Enhanced electron transfer and piezoelectricity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Engineering, Beijing Technology and Business University, Beijing 100048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Material Engineering, Beijing Technology and Business University, Beijing 100048, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of New South Wales, Sydney 2052, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of New South Wales, Sydney 2052, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Persulfate (PS) or peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activated sulfate radicals can significantly enhance advanced oxidation processes owing to their high oxidation potentials. However, highly efficient and continuous activation of PS or PMS has been limited owing to the sluggish redox cycle. Herein, a photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalyst, Carbon quantum dots modified MoS2 nanosheets (MoS2/C-dots), has been developed to boost the electron transfer for PMS activation, and achieved significant antibiotics degradation (85.06 % degradation under weak visible-light and simulated water flow within 10 min, which is 3.83 times higher than that with PMS). The advance of MoS2/C-dots fabrication is four-fold: a. the attached C-dots on MoS2 improved the asymmetry of MoS2 and enhanced its piezoelectricity, which accelerated the photogenerated e− and hole separation, and hence, improved its piezo-photocatalytic activity; b. C-dots surface charge promotes the electron transfer to MoS2, and provides e− for PMS activation; c. C-dots may act as e− banks for continuous PMS activation, hence, improving the PMS activation endurance; d. the photo-piezocatalysts can synergistically utilize the abundant weak visible light and the natural mechanical energy such as tide and water flow for highly efficient pollution degradation in wastewater and also exhibit good cycling stability. In addition, free radical trapping experiments were employed to reveal the PMS activation mechanism, and the results showed that h+, 1O2, SO4 ·−, and ·OH were the main active species and a possible reaction pathway has been proposed. This work provides a rational piezo-photocatalyst design strategy for high-efficiency PS or PMS activation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Advanced oxidation processes based on sulfate radicals (SO4 ·−) have been widely employed to degrade organic pollutants in wastewater owing to the mild reaction conditions, broad pH range, and more powerful oxidation (2.5 V–3.1 V vs NHE) [1,2]. SO4 ·− can be generated via persulfate (PS) or peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation by various approaches including thermally [3,4], transition metal ion activation [5], alkali activation [6,7], etc. However, these methods are limited by high energy consumption, long reaction times, and secondary pollution issues [8].

Moreover, ferrous iron (Fe (II)) activated PMS has the superiority of low cost, high activity, and environmental-friendly nature. For instance, in the Fe2+/PMS system, PMS can be effectively activated to generate sulfate radicals (SO4 ·−) by accepting e− from Fe2+ [9]. Unfortunately, the oxidized Fe3+ is difficult to be reduced back to Fe2+ in this system and the continuous consumption of Fe2+ greatly limited the endurance of PMS activation. Hence, it is critical to develop green and effective approaches for persulfate activation and continuous PMS activation is critical but difficult.

Recently, photocatalytic persulfate activation has been investigated as an effective and green method for organic degradations [10,11]. However, photocatalysis itself is also limited by the intermittent solar energy and weather conditions in nature, and suffering from the rapid recombination of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs [12]. Hence, to make full use of natural power including the sun and a wide range of vibration energy such as wind and wave energy, photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalysis has been developed in recent years [13].

When a piezoelectric material experiences external stress or strain, it causes spontaneous polarization for piezocatalysis and may also promote photogenerated e− and hole separation. The carriers can migrate to the surfaces of piezoelectric materials owing to their built-in electric field and easily capture oxygen/water to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) for removing pollutants [14,15]. Therefore, the development of a synergistic photo-piezocatalyst shows great potential in enhancing the activation of PMS [16,17].

MoS2, as a novel 2D material, is a recently developed piezo-photocatalytic catalyst for organic degradation owing to its narrow band gap, high surface electron concentration, ferroelectric properties, and large tolerance of elastic strains for structure deformation [18]. It has been reported the accelerated electron transfer in MoS2 under ultrasound can activate PMS for contaminant degradation [19,20]. However, compared to traditional piezoelectric materials such as BaTiO3, KxNa1−xNbO3, and Sr0.7Ba0.3Nb2O6, the piezoelectric performance of MoS2 needs to be further improved.

In our work, a synergistic piezo-photocatalyst of MoS2/ Carbon quantum dots (MoS2/C-dots) has been developed for efficient and continuous PMS activation, making full use of the natural solar and vibrational energy, achieving a high organic degradation rate. The Carbon quantum dots (C-dots) have the advantages of inexpensiveness, low toxicity, and good environmental compatibility and can be synthesized from natural carbon sources [21,22]. C-dots decorated MoS2 nanosheets have been synthesized via a simple one-pot hydrothermal process. The optimization of attaching the metal-free C-dots to the MoS2 is four-fold: a. the piezoelectric properties of MoS2 can be enhanced owing to the improved asymmetry; b. C-dots surface charge redistribution promotes the electron transfer to MoS2 and can achieve better PMS activation efficiency [23,24]; c. the C-dots with large π-conjugated bonds form a high-concentration electron zone, may act as an e− bank [25], ensured the continuous PMS activation; d. the improved synergistical photo-piezocatalytic activity enabled high efficient pollution degradation in wastewater, and the optimized catalyst also demonstrated good cyclic stability and environmental applicability.

Herein, antibiotics have been selected as target contaminants for investigation, since antibiotics have been widely used in various industries, many of them cannot be fully metabolized by humans and animals, resulting discharge of antibiotics into the environment, an increase in the category and content of refractory organic pollutants in water [26]. Various attempts have been investigated to remove antibiotics from wastewater, including adsorption [27,28], biological treatment [29,30] and membrane process [31], etc. However, their applications have been limited by the low removal efficiency, high energy consumption, and inability to effectively degrade antibiotics into small molecular organic compounds [32].

MoS2/C-dots activated PMS showed an excellent degradation rate and achieved almost complete degradation of tetracycline (TC) by 99.44 % within 60 min which is over 50 % higher than that with PMS only. Compared to the MoS2, owing to the higher asymmetric structure and better charge transfer between the MoS2 and C-dots, the MoS2/C-dots also show better catalytic efficiency, stability and cyclability. The catalyst is also suitable for the degradation of various antibiotics, including metronidazole (MDZ) and ciprofloxacin (CIP). The mechanism of MoS2 and MoS2/C-dots has been investigated and a radical-based reaction pathway has been proposed, which confirmed the critical role of h+, 1O2, SO4 ·− and ·OH in the MoS2/C-dots activated PMS system. Based on these results, we hope to provide a simple but effective strategy for designing and optimizing the metal-free 2D photo-piezocatalyst for PMS activation.

All the chemicals used in this work were analytically pure grade without further treatments. Citric acid monohydrate (C6H10O8), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2), Rhodamine B (RhB) and thiourea (CH4N2S) were all purchased from Sinopsin Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. Sodium molybdate (Na2MoO4), tetracycline (C22H24N2O8, TC), peroxymonosulfate (KHSO5, PMS), Potassium persulfate (K2S2O8, PS) metronidazole (C6H9N3O3, MDZ) and ciprofloxacin (CHFN3O3, CIP) were all purchased from Beijing Merida Technology Co., LTD.

First, 20 g C6H10O8 was dissolved in 7 mL deionized water and 6 mL C2H8N2 was slowly added into the solution. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined reactor and hydrothermal reacted at 180 °C for 20 h. After cooling down naturally, the obtained product was poured into anhydrous ethanol with stirring. Then the mixture was precipitated for 1–2 days, washed and centrifuged with anhydrous ethanol, and vacuum dried at 80 °C to obtain C-dots.

MoS2 and MoS2/C-dots were synthesized via the hydrothermal method. First, 1 g Na2MoO4 was dissolved in 30 mL deionized water and sonicated for 40 min. Followed by 0.6 g CH4N2S added and stirred for 30 min. Then 10 mL C-dots solution (0.7 mg/mL) was added and stirred for half an hour to obtain a highly uniform mixture. The solution was transferred into a Teflon reactor and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After naturally cooling down to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged, washed, and vacuum-dried at 70 °C to produce black MoS2/C-dots powder. Using the same method, without adding C-dots solution, black powder MoS2 was obtained. On this basis, MoS2/C-dots with different mass fractions [MoS2/wt% (C-dots) = 0.3 %, 0.5 %, 0.7 %, 0.9 %] were synthesized.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed by a Bruker AXS-D2 diffractometer (Karlsruhe, Germany) equipped with monochromatic high-intensity Cu-Ka radiation (λ = 0.154056 nm). Fourier transmission infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured on the Avatar 360 FT-IR spectrometer. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were performed using German Zeiss Sigma300 and American FEI Talos F200, respectively. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas were detected based on nitrogen adsorption isotherms, using an Autosorb-iQ (Quantachrome, USA). Optical properties were determined by Electrochemical workstation (CHI-760E) photoluminescence spectra (PL, Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer). Zeta potential measurement for synthesized samples was determined using a zeta analyzer (Horiba, SZ-100).

The photocatalysis, piezoelectric catalysis and photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalytic properties with different catalysts toward PMS activation were evaluated by degradation of antibiotics. First, the catalyst (45 mg) was dispersed in 100 mL antibiotic (12 mg/L) solution and stood in darkness for 20 min to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Then, a certain amount of PMS (50 mg) was added to the suspension, and the mixture was quickly subjected to different conditions to initiate PMS activation. For comparison, control experiments, including only PMS and only catalyst degradation of antibiotics under different conditions were also conducted. Visible light (JB400, 12.2 mW/cm2, 420 nm) has been employed as the light source in photocatalysis, and the distance between the light and the reaction solution was fixed at 10 cm. For piezoelectric catalysis measurements, an ultrasonic device (YM-030S, 40 kHZ, 80 W) was applied in the dark to avoid photocatalysis degradation. In the experiment of photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalysis degradation, the above light source was combined with an ultrasonic device for synergistic degradation. At a predetermined time, 5 mL solution was removed from the reaction suspension and the catalyst was filtered through a 0.22 µm aqueous syringe filter. The degradation curves of different antibiotics were tested by a WFJ-2000 spectrophotometer, where TC degradation was measured based on absorption at 352 nm, MDZ and CIP were tested at 300 nm and RhB was tested at 554 nm. Each experiment was repeated 3–5 times and averaged to ensure reproducibility.

SEM images were captured to characterize the morphology of the catalyst. Fig. 2 a–b shows the flower-like structure of MoS2/C-dots with a typical wrinkled and rippled structure, which is beneficial for exposing more active edge sites for inducing the piezoelectric polarization [33,36]. The elemental composition of the synthesized composite has been investigated using elemental mapping (Fig. 2c). EDS mapping shows the distribution of C, N, S and Mo elements in the MoS2/C-dots composite (Fig. 2d–h), which indicates that despite the relatively low C-dots content.

The nanostructures of MoS2 and MoS2/C-dots were further analyzed by TEM. As in Fig. 3 a, MoS2 has a lamellar structure without aggregation, which ensures a high surface area of the catalyst. The HRTEM image of MoS2 shows clear lattice fringes, where the lattice fringes of 0.273 nm and 0.250 nm correspond to the (100) and (102) crystal planes of MoS2, respectively (Fig. 3b). Lamellar structured MoS2 remained in the MoS2/C-dots (Fig. 3c), indicating the introduction of C-dots does not destroy the layer structure and distribution of MoS2. In Fig. 3d, C-dots are uniformly attached to the surface of MoS2, and the lattice fringe of 0.250 nm corresponds to the (102) crystal planes of MoS2.

Generally, the faster charge separation leads to a higher PMS activation capability. The transient photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were carried out to investigate the charge transport of the samples. The I-t curves of MoS2 and MoS2/C-dots under several switching cycles of simulated intermittent light exposure are shown in Fig. 4 a. Both MoS2 and MoS2/C-dots are show obvious photocurrent when the light is on and the photocurrent response still exists after several on/off light cycles. The higher photocurrent of MoS2/C-dots shows that it exhibits a stronger photocurrent response than MoS2. It is also worth noting that the photocurrent of MoS2/C-dots rises rapidly under light irradiation, while MoS2 rises slowly, which also indicates that MoS2/C-dots have a higher photogenerated carrier mobility compared to MoS2 [37]. In Fig. 4b, compared with MoS2, MoS2/C-dots exhibited the smallest arc in the EIS Nyquist plots, indicating the minimum charge transfer resistance in MoS2/C-dots. These results indicate that the fabrication of MoS2/C-dots is more efficient in electron-hole separation and photocatalytic reaction, and more conducive to the transmission of photogenerated carriers [38,39].

In addition, PL was also used to study the photocarrier separation efficiency of the catalysts. The lower photoluminescence intensity indicates that the photoexcited electron-hole pair separation efficiency and the photocatalytic behaviour are higher [40]. In Fig. 4c, the PL emission intensity of MoS2/C-dots is significantly reduced compared to the pure MoS2. The PL signal of MoS2/C-dots shows that the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs is effectively inhibited, which indicates that the formation of MoS2/C-dots is beneficial to promote the separation and transfer of photocarriers and improve the photocatalytic activity [41,42].

To investigate the best C-dots loaded ratio, the piezoelectric catalytic performance of MoS2/C-dots with different mass ratios was evaluated by degradation RhB solution (Fig. S3). The details are provided in the ESI. With stirring, over 80 min, the degradation rate of RhB with MoS2 is 43.1 %. Under the same conditions, with the increase of C-dots quality introduced into the system, the catalytic performance of the composite further increases. When the ratio reaches 0.7 %, the degradation rate of RhB is up to 63.1 %, which is about 1.46 times higher than MoS2. However, the catalytic performance decreases with the further increase of C-dots content. This phenomenon is due to the electrons from the large π-conjugated system of C-dots transferred to MoS2, decreasing the amount of S4+ and increasing the amount of S2 2− and S2−, which increases the highly active edge sites [43]. In addition, the introduction of an appropriate number of C-dots increases the asymmetry of the MoS2, and enhances its piezoelectric properties, while excessive C-dots will cause aggregation on the surface of MoS2 and reduce the active site for catalysis. Therefore, an appropriate C-dots content is essential to optimize the catalytic performance of lamellar MoS2.

Fig. 5 shows the TC degradation efficiency with pristine and C-dots loaded MoS2 under various conditions. First, we examined the adsorption properties of MoS2 and MoS2/C-dots under dark conditions. The results showed that the adsorption capacity of MoS2/C-dots was similar to that of MoS2 after 120 min. From Fig. S2, it can be seen that the BET data of MoS2 and MoS2/C-dots are similar and only a small number of C-dots are attached to MoS2, so the difference in adsorption capacity before and after optimization is not significant. We compared the degradation of TC under weak visible light, ultrasound (simulated night or cloudy days in a river or ocean with wave energy stimuli) and weak visible light irradiation with ultrasound (simulated sunny days with wave energy stimuli). Both MoS2 and MoS2/C-dots show low degradation activity under weak visible light irradiation (Fig. 5a). After 120 min, the degradation rate of TC with MoS2 was ∼24.33 % and MoS2/C-dots (28.67 %) was slightly higher, which proves that the fabrication of MoS2/C-dots can slightly promote photocatalytic activity owing to the electron transfer between C-dots and MoS2. Owing to the piezoelectric properties of MoS2 nanoflower, both MoS2 and MoS2/C-dots show good piezoelectric catalytic activity under ultrasonic compared to their photocatalytic activity. At 120 min, the degradation rate of TC with MoS2 and MoS2/C-dots is 78.59 % and 83.93 %, respectively. The synergistic piezo-photocatalytic performance of MoS2 and MoS2/C-dots has been significantly improved, confirming the degradation ability of TC in rivers or oceans during the daytime. After 120 min, the degradation rates of TC reached 82.5 % and 91.3 %, respectively. To provide a more direct comparison between degradation efficiency with different catalysts, we quantitatively gave the degradation rate: k = ln(C0/C)/t, where C0 is the initial concentration of TC, C is the concentration of TC at time t, and all fits are second-order fits. As in Fig. 5b, all the slopes of MoS2/C-dots are higher than those of MoS2 under each condition. Besides the improvements in the degradation efficiency by loading C-dots, the photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalysis degradation rate of TC with MoS2 decreased after 120 min, while MoS2/C-dots still maintains a higher catalytic rate, indicating the better endurance of catalytic activity of MoS2/C-dots than MoS2. This is because the decoration of MoS2 with C-dots improved the asymmetry of MoS2 and enhanced its piezoelectric properties, enabling efficient and stable photogenerated charge separation. The high surface area of C-dots also increased the contact between the carriers and TC, then improved the catalytic activity. Under photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalysis, the degradation rate of MoS2/C-dots is 3.18 times and 1.1 times higher than that under weak visible light irradiation and ultrasonic irradiation, respectively.

Based on the above results, we further studied the PMS activity efficiency with each catalyst for antibiotics degradation. The influence of PMS concentration on the activation system was explored and provided in Fig. S4. Herein, the PMS of 0.5 g/L was employed for further experiments. Fig. 6 a compared the MoS2 and MoS2/C-dots for PMS activation under weak visible light irradiation toward TC degradation. After 60 min, the degradation efficiency of MoS2 + PMS and MoS2/C-dots + PMS was much better than that with only PMS, which reached 55.57 % and 59.71 %, respectively. Under ultrasonic vibration as in Fig. 6b, after 60 min, MoS2/C-dots + PMS can almost completely degrade TC (99.51 %), which doubles the TC degradation efficiency with only PMS, indicates the endurance of PMS activation by MoS2/C-dots. With the synergistic stimuli of weak visible light and ultrasound, the catalyst and PMS show even better TC degradation. After 60 min, both MoS2 + PMS and MoS2/C-dots + PMS can almost completely degrade TC, 98.17 % and 99.44 %, respectively (Fig. 6c).

The degradation rates have been further compared in Fig. 6a*–c*, with MoS2/C-dots + PMS as a third-order fit and the rest as second-order fits. The introduction of C-dots improved the catalytic activity of MoS2 and PMS activation under all conditions, especially when ultrasound was applied. Compared with PMS, MoS2/C-dots, under the catalysis of piezoelectric and photo-piezoelectric, the improvement of PMS has reached 6.05 times and 4.15 times, respectively. This is because the loading of C-dots enhanced the piezoelectrical properties of MoS2, the internal electric field-induced polarization of MoS2/C-dots promoted the electron-hole separation and the C-dots also acted as the e− bank for PMS activation [43]. According to Refs. [44,45], the degradation rate measured by a UV–vis spectrophotometer and the TC change by a TOC monitor is mostly the same. We also evaluated the PS activation efficiency for TC degradation (Fig. S5). Fig. S5a compared the MoS2 and MoS2/C-dots for PS activation under weak visible-light irradiation and ultrasonic vibration toward TC degradation. After 60 min, both MoS2 + PS and MoS2/C-dots + PS can degrade TC efficiently by 84.75 % and 93.36 %, respectively. As for MoS2/C-dots + PS, the degradation efficiency was improved by 2.1 times compared to the case of PS only (44.60 %). The fitted rate curves are compared in Fig. S5b, and the significantly higher slope of the MoS2/C-dots + PS indicated better efficiency and endurance of PS activation.

Other than the catalytic efficiency, the endurance and recyclability of the catalysts are also critical for their practical applications. Therefore, the recycling performance is investigated in Fig. 7 a and b. MoS2/C-dots + PMS showed excellent endurance and catalytic activity after four consecutive cycles, while the degradation efficiency with MoS2 + PMS decreased slightly after each cycle. This may be related to the recovery of the membrane after filtration. In addition, the Zeta potential was measured to further analyze the stability of the sample in water (Fig. S6). To evaluate the structural stability, the MoS2 and MoS2/C-dots have been collected after the cycling tests and measured by XRD to compare with the pristine catalysts. As illustrated in Fig. S7, the XRD patterns of catalysts before and after the cycling tests show no obvious differences, indicating the stability of MoS2 and MoS2/C-dots over the reactions. In real life, TC usually coexists with organic matter and proteins naturally dissolver in domestic wastewater, thus Humic acid (HA) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were selected. Fig. 7c and d show that the degradation efficiency of TC decreased after the addition of HA and BSA, but the removal yield still reached 91 % and 95 % (MoS2 + PMS), 94 % and 98 % (MoS2/C-dots + PMS). This shows that the effect of organic matter on the catalytic system is in the acceptable range, probably due to the competitive interaction of HA and BSA with the oxidative radicals, which attenuates the degradation of TC [46].

To evaluate the anti-interference ability of the MoS2 before and after decorated by the C-dots, the TC degradations have been measured with various common anions and at different pH values. The effects of three common inorganic anions, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, and Cl−, have been explored in Fig. 8 a–b, and both MoS2 and MoS2/C-dots maintained high catalytic activity. It is noteworthy that the TC degradation decreased with the introduction of CO3 2−, which is attributed to the fact that CO3 2− can act as a scavenger of ·OH and thus the catalytic activity is limited [47]. In addition, the degradations under different pH 3.5, 4.6, 6.3 and 9.4 have been compared in Fig. 8c–d. Although both catalysts present high pH tolerance during the degradations, the C-dots decorated MoS2 show better catalytic efficiency, which indicates that the optimized catalyst is not sensitive to pH and can adapt to a wider range of pH environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism analysis of reactive species based on free radicals
ce:section.ce:para: The above experimental results indicate that metal-free MoS2/C-dots show excellent synergistic photo-piezoelectric activities and excellent PMS activation ability. To identify the main active species for TC degradation, active species capture experiments were performed. Details are provided in the ESI. Five scarves, including Ethanol (for ·OH and SO4 ·− trapping), tert-butanol TBA (for ·OH trapping), EDTA-2Na (for h+ trapping), ascorbate L-AA (for O2 ·− trapping) and furfuryl alcohol FFA (for 1O2 trapping), have been employed to identify the main active substances in each catalytic process [48]. As shown in Fig. 9 a, Ethanol, TBA, EDTA-2Na, L-AA and FFA all captured the active species, and the priority of active species was as follows: O2 ·− > 1O2 > h+ > ·OH > SO4 ·−, indicating that O2 ·− is the main free radical in MoS2 + PMS under photocatalytic process, and other active species may also participate in the degradation of TC, but the effect is limited. Similarly, O2 ·− is the dominant active species for MoS2/C-dots + PMS systems under photocatalytic process (Fig. 9d). In the MoS2 + PMS piezoelectric catalysis process (Fig. 9b), with the presence of EDTA-2Na, FFA and Ethanol, the degradation rate of TC decreased to 50.28 %, 59.69 % and 59.03 %, respectively, indicating that h+ played a key role than 1O2 in this system. It is worth noting that the introduction of TBA shows an effect on the degradation efficiency, while the introduction of Ethanol significantly reduces the catalytic efficiency, proving that SO4 ·− is effective, which is consistent with the literature [19]. In addition, in the system of MoS2/C-dots + PMS piezoelectric catalysis (Fig. 9e), the L-AA also had a certain inhibitory effect on the degradation of TC, but h+ still plays a more important role. For photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalysis (Fig. 9c and f), the addition of EDTA-2Na, Ethanol, FFA and TBA in both systems significantly inhibited the degradation of TC, indicating that the main active species in both systems are h+, 1O2, SO4 ·−, and ·OH, other active substances inhibited the degradation efficiency of TC to a certain extent, but their contribution was modest. The above results indicate that the main actors of MoS2 after loading C-dots in the presence of both weak visible light and ultrasound are h+, 1O2, SO4 ·−, and ·OH.

We comparatively analyzed the SO4 2− radical generation rate of C-dots before and after the optimization of MoS2, and the C-dot optimization improved the SO4 2− generation efficiency by a factor of 1.33 (Fig. 10 a). To further illustrate, the EPR spin-trapping technique was employed for the detection of SO4 2−. The results were consistent with free radical scavenging experiments. After ultrasound treatment for 10 min, MoS2/C-dots + PMS shows more stronger SO4 2− signal than MoS2 + PMS (Fig. 10b), which indicated that MoS2 optimized by C-dots is more conducive to activating PMS to produce SO4 2−. As shown in Fig. 10c, the h+ signal is greatly decreased under ultrasound, indicating the generation of holes both in MoS2 + PMS and MoS2/C-dots + PMS. Moreover, the signal of MoS2/C-dots + PMS is weaker than that of MoS2 + PMS after ultrasound treatment for 10 min, indicating more holes were collected on the surface of MoS2/C-dots [49]. This shows that the piezoelectric properties are enhanced after C-dots are attached to MoS2, and the e−/h+ separation efficiency is further improved. In addition, as illustrated in Fig. 10d and e, MoS2/C-dots also exhibit stronger O2 ·− and 1O2 signals (agreed with Fig. 9) than MoS2, indicating a more reactive radical generation efficiency after the C-dots modification [50].

To understand the reaction pathway, our experimental results have been compared to the DFT calculations in literature. The electronic structure of MoS2/C-dots and the PMS activation based on MoS2 have been previously reported. From the aspect of electronic structures, the attachment of C-dots to MoS2 triggered the surface charge density redistribution and broke its symmetry [25], which may enhance the piezoelectricity of MoS2. The electron-enriched area is located around the C-dots and the electron transfer from C-dots to MoS2 is observed, which may improve the catalytic activity [25,51]. To simulate the piezoelectric catalysis, Liu et al. have calculated the PMS activation based on MoS2 with different tensile strains applied [19]. The band gap of MoS2 decreases under piezoelectric stress, which promotes electron-hole generation and separation, and accelerates the activation of PMS. Together with our EPR, radical scavenging experimental results and previous experimental reports [52], a possible reaction pathway has been proposed in Equation (1)–(9) and the corresponding catalytic mechanism is schematically illustrated in Fig. 11 . Two possible degradation pathways of TC have been proposed based on previous studies [44,53]: TC can be demethylated and dehydroxylated for further cleavage and eventually mineralized into CO2 and H2O, or it is deprotonated through the process of loss of N-dimethyl groups and hydroxyl groups to produce deprotonated product that are further dihydroxylation process and the loss of methyl groups for mineralization to CO2 and H2O.

Under the synergistic stimuli of weak visible light irradiation and ultrasound, the internal electric field in MoS2/C-dots drives the photogenerated electrons and holes migration towards the opposite direction, generating a large number of charges at the surface of catalysts (Equation (1)). Then a large number of surface charges and holes attack the OO bond of PMS for PMS activation. The e− accumulated on the catalyst surface not only reacts with PMS to form SO4 ·− and ·OH (Equation (2)), but also reduces O2 to O2 ·− (Equation (3)) [54]. At the same time, h+ on the surface reacts with PMS and H2O to form PMS anionic free radicals (SO5 ·−) and ·OH (Equations (4) and (5)) [19]. The two SO5 ·− ions can spontaneously combine to form SO4 ·− and 1O2 (Equation (6)), then, SO4 ·− can easily extract hydrogen atoms from water to produce ·OH (Equation (7)) [48]. In addition, ·OH and O2 ·− can further react to form 1O2 (Equation (8)) [39]. These powerful active substances then split the TC molecules into inorganic compounds as well as CO2, and H2O (Equation (9)), to achieve the purpose of degradation.

O + i n o r g a n i c
c o m p o u n d s

To examine the applicability of the MoS2/C-dots, we investigated the heterostructure catalyst in degrading other antibiotics under the same conditions. It can be seen from Fig. 12 a and b that under the photo-piezoelectric synergetic catalysis, after 30 min, the degradation rates of MDZ and CIP are 95.9 % and 86.5 %, respectively, showing excellent degradation activities, which indicates the catalytic potential of the system for a variety of pollutants.

In summary, MoS2/C-dots with enhanced piezoelectricity and electron transfer were designed and prepared via a simple hydrothermal method for boosting the PMS activation. High-efficiency antibiotic degradation has been achieved by coupling photo-piezoelectric synergetic catalysis and PMS activation. Within 10 min, 85.06 % TC degradation has been achieved even with very weak visible-light irradiation and simulated wave energy. The free radical capture experiments showed that h+, 1O2, SO4 ·−, and ·OH were dominant free radicals in the MoS2/C-dots activated PMS processes. This study proves that the decoration of MoS2 with C-dots can greatly enhance its piezoelectricity, promoting electron transfer, improving the activation efficiency of PMS, and hence, achieving effective pollution degradation in wastewater. This work provides a simple but efficient strategy for synergistic piezo-photocatalyst design and also the pathway to promote PMS or PS activation for environmental remediation under various natural water conditions.

Shule Zhang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhemi Xu: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Tianhao Ji: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Peiyuan Guan: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Data curation. Yunxuan Weng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Data curation.

Weng acknowledge financial support from Research on Plastic Biodegradation Mechanism and Establishment of Consistency Evaluation System in Different Water Environments (Grant No. 52073004).
